title: North Fork
subtitle: Eden, Utah
summary:
## The Crew

Bradley, Dad, Perrin, and Keishin. Four guys, one hilltop, zero regrets (mostly).

## The Hike In

Four miles in on the North Fork River Trail out of Eden, Utah. Spring was in full swing — snowmelt had the river running hard, and the crew crossed water in multiple spots on the way in. Nobody complained. The canyon views through the pines were doing a lot of heavy lifting.

Keishin slipped on the trail three times. Not once. Not twice. Three times. He's fine. It's fine. It was immediately hilarious.

## Camp

The hilltop site earned its reputation the moment we crested it. After a trail that had spent most of its time throwing inclines at us, the camp was flat, level, and open — a rare find. Rocky? Yes. But that's what sleeping pads are for.

No big trees up here, which means nothing blocking the view in any direction. To the north, a hulking hill dominates the skyline. To the south, the canyon opens up all the way back to the trailhead — you can actually see the parking lot from up here. Pine View Reservoir glittered in the distance. Snow still clung to the southwest peaks. On a clear night, the city lights leaked in from the valley below.

The two camp chairs were an absolutely inspired decision. Highly recommended. 10/10. Put them on the permanent list.

## The Bear Can Incident

So. The chicken. Raw chicken seemed like a good idea before the trip. It was not a good idea on the trip. The packaging ruptured inside the bear can, sending chicken juice across everything it touched — including Keishin's hands and Bradley's jacket. The irony of using a bear can to contain the smell of raw chicken while also smelling like raw chicken was not lost on anyone.

Lesson learned: cook the chicken at home. Freeze it. Seal it properly. Future-you will thank present-you.

## Water Situation

The LifeStraw came through. Perrin and Keishin headed back down the trail to filter water from the river — the source ended up being farther than expected, so the group made do with what they had. Enough to cook with, enough to drink. Next time: filter early and often.

## Wildlife

One bird. About a foot tall. Ground-dwelling, didn't linger. Best guess: chukar partridge, possibly a pheasant. Either way, it had places to be.

## Lessons Learned

- **Pre-cook the chicken.** Raw protein in a bear can is a recipe for chaos and regret.
- **Double-bag anything liquid.** Assume it will leak. It will leak.
- The spice kit exists. It lives in a camping container. Pack it.
- Collapsible bowls — we needed them, we didn't have them.
- Hot chocolate should be considered a survival item at this elevation.
- Bring a thermometer — knowing the overnight temp is useful data.
- Thicker sleeping pads would be a step up on rocky terrain.
- Perrin's sleeping pad is cooked. Needs replacing before the next trip.
- A repair kit has been added to the checklist. It wasn't here before. It is now.
- Ground tarp: bring one.

## Final Verdict

Banger of a trip. Great views, solid crew, and enough lessons learned to make the next one even better. The hilltop site is worth coming back to.
